votes.reason: The Python code faithfully translates the Coq specification, correctly implementing natural number subtraction, character retrieval with bounds checking, and the cyclic encoding logic for all indices.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq specification, correctly handling natural number subtraction, character retrieval with out-of-bounds padding, and the specific cyclic encoding logic.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq specification, including the use of natural number subtraction and the custom get_char function to handle out-of-bounds indices.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks length equality, computes n using natural number subtraction (where 0-1=0), and for each index i: if i<=n, it verifies either cond1 ((i+1)%3==1 and output[i]==input[i+2]) or cond2 ((i+1)%3==2 or 0 and output[i]==input[i-1] with nat subtraction); if i>n, it verifies output[i]==input[i]. The logic matches the Coq spec's disjunction structure and nat_sub handles Coq's natural number subtraction semantics correctly.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks length equality, computes n using natural number subtraction (nat_sub handles the 0-1=0 case), and for each index i: if i <= n, it verifies either cond1 ((i+1)%3=1 and output[i]=input[i+2]) or cond2 ((i+1)%3=2 or 0 and output[i]=input[i-1] with nat subtraction); if i > n, it verifies output[i]=input[i]. The disjunction structure in Python (cond1 or cond2) matches the Coq specification's logical structure for the i <= n case.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks length equality, computes n using natural number subtraction (where 0-1=0), and for each index i: if i<=n, it verifies either cond1 ((i+1)%3==1 and output[i]==input[i+2]) or cond2 ((i+1)%3==2 or 0 and output[i]==input[i-1] with nat subtraction); if i>n, it verifies output[i]==input[i]. The logic matches the Coq spec's disjunction structure and nat_sub handles Coq's natural subtraction semantics correctly.

The Python code mirrors the Coq spec: it enforces equal lengths, uses n = ((L//3)*3)-1 with nat-style subtraction, applies the same (i+1) mod 3-based mapping for i ≤ n, keeps characters unchanged for i > n, and mimics Coq’s get_char with space for out-of-bounds. Thus it matches the concrete behavior on all inputs.

Python mirrors Coq on length check, n via nat subtraction, space-padding get_char, and the conditional mapping for i<=n vs i>n using the same modulo cases; thus it enforces the same output for all inputs.
Python mirrors Coq: same length check, same n via nat-subtraction, same per-index implications using get_char with space fallback, and identical mod-3 case handling.
